data.title: REITs Rally As Earnings Begin
data.description:
Real Estate Weekly Outlook

U.S. equity markets extended their winning streak into a sixth week - while benchmark interest rates posted weekly declines for the first time since the September rate cut - as a sharp retreat in oil prices calmed near-term inflation worries, while a busy slate of corporate earnings results and economic data were broadly consistent with a continuing "soft landing" economic trajectory.

Closing the week at fresh record-highs, the S&P 500 gained another 0.9% this week - posting gains for the eleventh week in the past twelve. The "value rotation" theme was back this week after a month-long reversal, with gains led by rate-sensitive segments and smaller-cap domestic companies - the laggards throughout the Fed's hiking cycle. The Small-Cap 600 rallied 1.7% on the week, while the Mid-Cap 400 gained 1.4%, each significantly outpacing the 0.2% gains on the tech-heavy Nasdaq 100. Real estate equities were notable leaders this week - snapping a five-week streak of underperformance - buoyed by a solid start to earnings season and a slight pull-back in benchmark rates. The Equity REIT Index rallied 2.9% this week, with 16-of-18 property sectors in positive territory. The Mortgage REIT Index gained 3.1%, while Homebuilders surged nearly 6%.

Helping to ease some concerns of firming inflation on the heels of lukewarm CPI and PPI data last week, WTI Crude Oil plunged nearly 8% this week - dipping back below $70/barrel - on a combination of weak European and Chinese economic data and indications of modestly easing tensions in the Middle East. After jumping above $77 in early October, oil prices are again trading negative on the year and are back within 5% of three-year lows. Soft trends across Europe and Asia have helped to contain some of the interest rate uplift over the past month from a brighter outlook on the U.S. consumer sparked by a series of solid employment reports. Retreating from its highest levels since July 31st, the 10-Year Treasury Yield declined 3 basis points on the week to 4.08% - up from its mid-September lows of 3.62% but still down from its 2024 highs of 4.71%. The policy-sensitive 2-Year Treasury Yield declined by 1 basis point to 3.95%. Swaps markets now price-in 0.90 rate cuts in November (unchanged from last week) and project a total of 3.75 rate cuts in 2024 (including the 2 in September) - down slightly from 3.84 last Friday.

Real Estate Economic Data

Below, we recap the most important macroeconomic data points over this past week affecting the residential and commercial real estate marketplace.

Consistent with the theme of the past several months, economic data and corporate news flow this past week lacked a clear-cut narrative, revealing ongoing pockets of softness in manufacturing and housing market activity, but also notable resilience in labor market trends and consumer activity. A key look into the health and sentiment of the U.S. consumer, Retail Sales were marginally better than expected in September, highlighted by the strongest Core Retail Sales (ex-Auto/Gas) figure in three months. Total Retail Sales rose 0.4% during the month and 1.7% from a year earlier, topping estimates of 0.3% and 1.6%, respectively. Ten of the report's 13 categories posted increases - led by clothing, health/personal care, and grocery stores - which offset a drag from lower gasoline and auto sales. Employment data was solid as well, as closely watched weekly Jobless Claims figures showed a pullback in initial claims following a Hurricane-related surge in the prior week. Manufacturing data was notably soft, however, with Industrial Production declining in September amid headwinds from a series of labor union strikes.

Equity REIT & Homebuilder Week In Review

Best & Worst Performance This Week Across the REIT Sector

Office: An office revival? The top-performing property sector this year with gains of nearly 40%, office REITs extended their rebound this week as the initial slate of earnings results showed a continued recovery in office space demand. NYC-focused SL Green (SLG) - the fourth-largest office REIT - surged more than 16% this week after it kicked off office REIT earnings season with a solid report highlighted by impressive leasing activity. SLG signed 764k SF of Manhattan office space in the third quarter - its largest quarterly activity in two years - which was roughly 40% above its average pre-pandemic level from 2017-2019. Just as impressive, SLG signed these leases at rents that were 10.8% higher than its prior fully escalated rents on the same space - its second-straight quarter of double-digit leasing spreads. The momentum continued into the third quarter as well: SLG announced that it signed a massive 925k SF renewal and expansion lease with Bloomberg, LP at 919 Third Avenue. The deal includes an 11-year lease covering 749k SF in its existing footprint and a 15-year lease covering a 175k SF expansion. As a result of this activity, SLG significantly boosted its full-year guidance for leasing activity to 3.3M SF - up from its prior target of 2.0M - and increased its occupancy target to 92.5% - up from 91.6% previously.

Elsewhere in the office space, Sunbelt-focused Piedmont (PDM) rallied nearly 12% this week after it provided preliminary third-quarter operating metrics showing a continued recovery in office demand. PDM signed 461k sf of leasing during the third quarter of 2024 - a slowdown from the impressive Q2 bookings of over 1M SF - but still a decent haul that is within 10% of its pre-pandemic averages. This leasing activity helped to raise its in-service leased percentage to 88.8%, up from 87.3% at the end of Q2. PDM noted that its contractual backlog stands at 1.5 million square feet of leased space yet to commence, representing approximately $48 million of future annual cash flow. As noted in our Earnings Preview, brokerage firm JLL (JLL) released its quarterly office update last week, which showed very encouraging trends for the long-struggling office sector. Nationally, third-quarter leasing activity maintained strong momentum from Q2, growing 0.4% quarter-over-quarter to 50.4M SF. Leasing over the past six months reflects 86% of pre-pandemic activity levels. JLL reports that office market availability declined for the first time in over five years - an important milestone - citing a concurrent acceleration in leasing activity and the slowdown of new supply.

Industrial: We saw results from a trio of industrial REITs this week, which painted a mixed picture regarding the state of logistics-related space demand, indicating some continued near-term softening in demand and lingering supply headwinds - but not the sharp, widespread drop-off that some had feared. Prologis (PLD) - the largest logistics property owner in the nation - kicked off earnings season with a surprisingly solid report - and Sunbelt-focused First Industrial (FR) reported even stronger trends - but West Coast-focused Rexford (REXR) followed with a downbeat report later in the week. Sector stalwart Prologis gained 2% after it maintained its property-level metrics and marginally raised its outlook for full-year Core FFO - now expecting 8.0% growth this year. PLD reported solid leasing activity, achieving an impressive cash leasing spread of 44.1% - down slightly from 51.4% in Q2 - while noting that much of this rent slowdown is attributable to weakness in Southern California. Rexford - which focuses exclusively on this region - dipped 3% on the week after lowering its full-year NOI growth outlook and noted that SoCal market rents fell another 2.5% during the quarter and were roughly 7.5% lower from a year earlier, which resulted in a sharp cooldown in rent spreads to 26.7% in Q3 from 49.0% last quarter. REXR cited "increased levels of global unrest, uncertainty related to the presidential election, and an uncertain economic outlook" in driving a "slower" pace of activity.

Sunbelt-focused First Industrial gained 1.5% after reporting decent results - raising its full-year NOI and FFO outlook driven by impressive rent spreads, but trimmed its occupancy outlook following an unexpected vacancy of a 1.1M SF facility in Pennsylvania leased to U.K.-based retailer Boohoo. Underscoring the continued embedded upside from mark-to-market rent adjustments, FR achieved impressive cash rent spreads of 66.8% - its second-best quarter on record and up from 43% last quarter - and FR now expects full-year FFO growth of 7.8% - up from 7.0% previously. Macro commentary was relatively upbeat, conceding that activity-levels nationwide have clearly softened from the robust pace from 2021-2023, but FR expects demand to pick-up after the "fog" from election uncertainty clears. FR noted that overall market rents are "up 2-3%" excluding Southern California, and "flattish to up a little bit" overall. We heard similar commentary from Prologis, which reiterated its call that fundamentals are "bottoming," and provided updated metrics that reflected expectations of soft fundamentals in the near-term followed by a recovery in 2025 as supply growth cools. Prologis slightly downgraded its net absorption forecast this year to 160M SF - down from 165M last quarter - and revised up its supply forecast to 300M - up from 290M. Prologis now expects a market-wide vacancy rate of 6.8% at the end of 2024 - up from 6.6% previously.

Cell Tower: Crown Castle (CCI) - the second-largest cell tower REIT - gained 1.5% this week after it reported in-line results and maintained its full-year outlook, but provided few incremental details on a rumored deal to sell its non-tower assets. Earlier this month, Reuters reported that CCI was nearing a potential deal to sell its fiber unit and potentially also its "small cell" unit, noting that Zayo Group and private equity firm TPG (TPG) are the final bidders. Crown Castle had expanded its fiber business through a series of acquisitions beginning in 2015, but the capital-intensive nature of building fiber infrastructure combined with its more-tower-focused peer's stock price outperformance has prompted calls from investors and activists to reconsider its strategy and focus on its core macro tower unit. CCI did not comment on reports, but did announce that it trimmed its small-cell deployment plans by 7,000 nodes at "locations that had countless zoning and permitting delays or in high-cost markets that did not meet our investment parameters." CCI still has 40k small-cell locations in its backlog - 70% of which are higher-yield colocation nodes as opposed to single-tenant anchor build. Commentary on carrier demand was status-quo, with CCI reiterating that demand is "steady state" following a significant decline in network spending last year. CCI maintained its outlook, which anticipates comparable revenue growth of 4.5% from towers, 15% from small cells, and 2% from fiber solutions.

Net Lease: Small-cap Alpine Income (PINE) - which owns 137 properties across 34 states - gained 3% this week after it kicked-off net lease earnings season with a solid report. Driven by recent "capital recycling" transactions, PINE now expects full-year AFFO growth of 14.1%, up from 8.7% previously. Consistent with its recent "selective pruning" of its portfolio by selling lower-yielding assets and recycling the proceeds into higher-yield assets, PINE noted that it acquired four net leased retail properties for $37.5M, at a weighted average initial cash cap rate of 8.8%, while selling eight properties for $48.6M at a weighted average exit cash cap rate of 6.8%. PINE noted that it continued to reduce its exposure to Walgreens (WBA) - previously its top tenant at 12% of ABR - which announced earlier this year that it is seeking to "significantly" reduce its store count. PINE sold two Walgreens during the quarter, which dropped its exposure to below 10%. Speaking on the macro environment, PINE remarked that it has seen an uptick in transaction velocity following a two-year lull during the Fed's hiking cycle, but indicated that it sees better risk-reward in the loan origination space - noting that it expects its loan portfolio to be about 10% of its total asset value going forward.

Hotels: On the downside this week, Service Properties (SVC) - one of four public REITs that are externally managed by asset manager RMR Group (RMR) - dipped 25% this week after it joined the four other RMR-advised REITs in slashing its dividend while concurrently announcing a deal to sell a significant slice of its portfolio in order to pay down debt. SVC - which operates with an elevated debt ratio of 82% - reduced its quarterly dividend by 95% to $0.01/share, noting that the dividend reduction will result in $127M of annual savings. SVC also announced plans to sell 114 hotels (14,925 rooms) - over half of its 220-property hotel portfolio - in order to reduce leverage, citing "deteriorating leverage metrics" due to a slow recovery of its hotel portfolio and significant renovation cap-ex needs. The hotels are managed by its RMR-managed affiliate - Sonesta International - and neither the buyer nor the price was disclosed, but SVC noted that the portfolio had $850M in aggregate book value. SVC noted that it had planned to spend $725M in capital expenditures over the next six years at these hotels. Since the end of 2019, the four RMR-advised REITs declined by an average of 75%. Office Properties Income (OPI) - which has dipped 90% since 2019 - slashed its dividend to a penny in January. Industrial Logistics Properties (ILPT) - which has dipped 76% - slashed its dividend to a penny in 2022. Diversified Healthcare (DHC) - which has dipped 52% - slashed its dividend to a penny in 2020.

This week, we published our REIT Earnings Preview, which discussed the major high-level themes and metrics we'll be watching across each property sector. The sector with the most upside from easing interest rates, REITs rebounded sharply ahead of the Fed's "jumbo" rate cut, but have fluctuated in October amid changing rate expectations. The "soft landing" trajectory has been particularly supportive of office and retail REITs - along with small-caps - each aching for a "Goldilocks" environment of lower rates and lukewarm growth. These macroeconomic conditions - combined with rebounding equity valuations - are ideal for a revival of the long-dormant "animal spirits" for public REITs through IPOs and acquisitions of debt-burdened private portfolios. Notable trends gleaned from recent data include: 1) gradually recovering office fundamentals; 2) anchored housing market trends despite historically low activity levels; 3) sustained hotel and leisure demand; 4) cooling in (currently robust) retail fundamentals; and 5) sluggish trends in "goods-oriented" sectors (industrial, farmland, timber). Below, we compiled the earnings calendar for equity REITs and homebuilders.

REIT Capital Raising Activity

It was a quiet week on the capital-raising front following a surge in activity in September and into early October. Community Healthcare (CHCT) amended and extended its $400M credit facility - increasing its total capacity by $150M and lowering its pricing by 10-30 basis points - which it used to pay off a separate $75M term loan facility. On the credit ratings front, a trio of office REITs received credit rating downgrades. S&P Ratings lowered its credit ratings on Kilroy Realty (KRC) to "BBB-" from "BBB" with a stable outlook; S&P lowered its rating on Highwoods (HIW) to "BBB-" from "BBB" with a stable outlook; and S&P lowered its issuer credit rating of Piedmont (PDM) to "BB+" from "BBB-" but affirmed its "BBB-" senior unsecured notes rating with a stable outlook. Meanwhile, S&P affirmed its ratings on Boston Properties (BXP) at "BBB" with a negative outlook. Elsewhere, Fitch Ratings affirmed its credit ratings on American Assets (AAT) at "BBB" with a stable outlook, and Fitch affirmed its credit ratings on Equinix (EQIX) at "BBB" with a stable outlook. Finally, Moody's affirmed the credit ratings of Sabra Health Care (SBRA) at "Ba1" and revised its outlook to positive from stable.

2024 Performance Recap

Performance trends have shifted dramatically over the two months, as the long-underperforming real estate sector has closed some of the historically wide gap with other segments of the equity market amid a sharp pull-back in benchmark interest rates. The Equity REIT Index is now higher by 13.6% this year, while the Mortgage REIT Index is higher by 6.7%. This compares with the 24.2% gain on the S&P 500, the 16.1% gain for the Mid-Cap 400, and the 10.2% gain for the Small-Cap 600. Within the REIT sector, 14 of the 18 property sectors are higher for the year, led by Cannabis, Office, Billboard, and Healthcare REITs, while Farmland, Hotel, and Industrial REITs have lagged on the downside. At 4.08%, the 10-Year Treasury Yield is now higher by 20 basis points on the year, while the 2-Year Treasury Yield has dipped by 48 basis points to 3.95%. The aggregate Bloomberg US Bond Index is now higher by 3.0% this year on a total return basis. In commodities markets, WTI Crude Oil is higher by 7.1% this year, while the broader Commodities complex is now higher by 4.2% on the year as higher oil prices have been partially offset by a dip in Natural Gas prices.

Economic Calendar In The Week Ahead

Housing markets will remain the focus of the economic calendar in the week ahead. We'll see both major home sales reports earlier in the week, which will provide good insight into where the housing market stands after two years of abnormally tight monetary policy. On Wednesday, Existing Home Sales data is expected to show an annualized sales velocity of 3.9M in September - still hovering around three-decade lows, and considerably below the typical 5.0-5.5M range. On Thursday, we'll see New Home Sales data, which is expected to show sales activity at a 710k annualized sales pace in September - roughly flat from a year ago - as builders continue to contend with financing and affordability issues. On Thursday, we'll also be watching the weekly Jobless Claims report for indications of a further deterioration in employment trends. On Friday, we'll see the revised October Consumer Sentiment data from the University of Michigan, which unexpectedly dipped in the preliminary report earlier this month amid renewed concerns over inflation and the economy.

For an in-depth analysis of all real estate sectors, check out all of our quarterly reports: Apartments, Homebuilders, Manufactured Housing, Student Housing, Single-Family Rentals, Cell Towers, Casinos, Industrial, Data Center, Malls, Healthcare, Net Lease, Shopping Centers, Hotels, Billboards, Office, Farmland, Storage, Timber, Mortgage, and Cannabis.

Disclosure: Hoya Capital Real Estate advises two Exchange-Traded Funds listed on the NYSE. In addition to any long positions listed below, Hoya Capital is long all components in the Hoya Capital Housing 100 Index and in the Hoya Capital High Dividend Yield Index. Index definitions and a complete list of holdings are available on our website.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Avis Budget Group Is Still Not A Buy Unless You Expect Plunging Interest Rates
data.description:
The stock price of Avis Budget Group, Inc. (NASDAQ:CAR) has been on a roller coaster ride over the past few years and some investors have recently decided to get back in the roller coaster car because they expect interest rates to drop. I am not one of those. I still think interest rates will remain higher for longer than some market bulls expect. While there have been a number of positive developments for Avis over the last few weeks, I still rate the stock a hold even at the current low price. This is an update to my prior CAR articles.

No Stock Repurchases This Year

First, I want to cover an issue that I covered in my February article that I considered at that time to be a major negative for CAR stock - the massive amount of money spent on what I considered irrational stock repurchases. Good news. In the first half of 2024 the company did not make any stock repurchases unlike in the first half of 2023 when they used $146.1 million to repurchase 723k shares at an average price of $202 per share compared to the current CAR price of $81.06.

It is unclear when, if ever, Avis will continue their stock repurchase program based on the statement by CFO Izzy Martins during their August conference call:

We firmly believe our stock is undervalued and that buybacks represent a compelling opportunity, as you can imagine, to create permanent value for our shareholders. However, with the general volatility that you see in the marketplace, and the potential implications of used car market, et cetera, I think we -- as we've always said, it's prudent to be patient allocators of our capital, especially in these uncertain times. So as always, we will deploy our cash flow where we believe it will make the most impact for the company and our stakeholders.

As of June 30 they still had board authorization to repurchase an additional $802 million shares. While management has not actually given specific reasons for the change, I think that the increase in the long-term debt at the corporate level to $5.233 billion as of June 30 from $4.791 billion at the end of last year and higher interest rates on refinancing debt may have been factors. Earlier this year they redeemed 4.75% notes with cash from a new 7.0% note sale. A few weeks ago they also sold 8.25% notes. The days of borrowing cheap and using that cash to repurchase shares are over, in my opinion.

Potential Positive News

As I mentioned in my September Hertz Global Holdings (HTZ) article, there was a little bit of positive recent news regarding used vehicle prices according to the Manheim Used Vehicle Value Index. The positive news has actually improved since that article was published. "Wholesale used-vehicle prices (on a mix-, mileage-, and seasonally adjusted basis) increased 0.3% from September in the first 15 days of October" according to Manheim. The unadjusted price dropped 1.7%, but October is often a weak month because next year models become available for consumers to buy and it is the end of the summer driving season.

Another modest bright spot is the October 10 CPI data. Used car and truck prices increased 0.3% from the prior month on a seasonally adjusted basis (down 0.1% non-seasonally adjusted), but the prices were down 5.1% from September 2023. Car and truck rental prices increased 1.3% from prior monthly on a seasonally adjusted basis (down 4.9% non-seasonally adjusted), but down 6.8% from September 2023.

The price declines varied significantly by vehicle type. EVs had the worst decline, but Avis did not buy a massive EV fleet unlike Hertz that has been seriously hurt because of their large prior EV purchases.

Another positive for car rental companies is that TSA airport screening numbers were higher this past summer compared to last year, but the last few weeks TSA numbers have been lower on a number of days compared to last year. I assume that the two hurricanes may have had an impact. It will be interesting to see if the storms caused a large number of damaged vehicles resulting in potentially higher demand for vehicles, either new or used, and potentially higher regional vehicle prices. I also wonder how many, if any, of their vehicles were damaged. Hopefully, much of the damage will be cleared up before the start of the Florida winter travel season starts in a few weeks or 4Q results could be negatively impacted.

Understanding the Impact of Depreciation

Most readers seem to understand how high interest rates have a negative impact on Avis - it is more expensive to refinance debt and more expensive to finance the purchase of new vehicles. There is, of course, another impact. Used vehicle prices are often lower when interest rates are high and potential buyers are less willing to pay higher prices. Based on comments I read on various websites there still is some confusion by retail investors regarding how these used vehicle prices/values are reflected in the company's income statement. There is a line in their income statement - "Vehicle depreciation and lease charges, net". (I wish GAAP accounting would require the details behind this "net" number in the actual income statement, but it does not.) The details are in the footnotes.

2Q and Six Month Income Statement 2024 and 2023

Details Behind the Numbers

The first item is "depreciation expense". This is not like typical depreciation where it is only an accounting entry number - it is an actual cash payment that is part of their monthly lease cash payment to AESOP, which is the remote ABS entity that actually owns their vehicles. The reduction in their vehicle resale value is the depreciation, which can be adjusted to reflect changes in the market. The terms of the lease agreement covers how this number is determined specifically for Avis - it is not determined by some GAAP standard. (In a prior article I covered the differences in determining the depreciation factor for Avis and Hertz at the start of the pandemic and why Hertz went bankrupt and Avis did not.) If the used vehicle market has weak prices, the depreciation amount is higher and the reverse is true when the prices are strong. Partially because the used vehicle prices have been weaker than last year, the depreciation expense has increased.

The entry "(Gain) loss on sale of vehicles, net" often confuses investors. The gain/loss they understand, but the cause of the large variation from one period to another can be confusing. If the depreciation factors, which again are determined under the terms of the lease agreement, are too high - in other words the vehicle book value is too low compared to what a vehicle actually sold for - a gain is reported. The reverse results in a loss. In 2Q' 23 they reported a $205 million gain, which implies that in prior reporting periods the depreciation factors were way too high, which means reported income was effectively too low. Gains reduce the monthly cash lease payments and losses increase the payments. These gains/losses can have a dramatic impact on reported income results.

If interest rates dropped sharply over the next year, used vehicle prices most likely will be more robust, unless the rate decline is due to a recession. With lower rates eventually the depreciation expense per vehicle will be lower and it is more likely that Avis will be able to sell their used vehicles at above their book value, which would result in gains. Collectively these two factors could lower their net vehicle depreciation and lease charges. This would increase income and improve their cash flow.

Since I don't expect interest rates to drop sharply over the next year, I disagree with the basis for the above bullish expectation. I think rates will remain higher for longer. I am, therefore, not buying CAR stock.

Use Cash Flow Metric

I prefer to use trailing twelve month - TTM cash flow from operations instead of GAAP earnings or EBITDA when looking at CAR stock price because I feel it directly relates to actual operations. The direction of cash flow has been going the wrong direction lately and the stock price has followed.

If you compare historical cash flow from operations and total equity capitalization, instead of just stock price, it seems that CAR is a "little cheap", but before I buy I want it to be "very cheap" and not just a little.

(Remember the "I" and the "D" in EBITDA for Avis Budget Group relates only to their corporate interest expenses and depreciation - they do not include the interest and depreciation expenses for their vehicles.)

Conclusion

Since CAR is somewhat undervalued based on the current total equity capitalization and cash flow from operations compared to historical numbers, investors may want to consider buying the stock if, and only if, they expect interest rates to drop significantly over the next year without the economy heading into a recession. For those of us who expect interest rates to remain higher for longer, Avis Budget Group stock continues to be a "hold".

While I am glad that the company has not repurchased any shares so far this year, I worry that they will return to the repurchase plan if/when operations and cash flow improves. I would not buy CAR stock if they restart repurchasing stock even if interest rates drop because the company is so highly leveraged.

data.title: Eli Lilly Buy On Weakness Signal (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) just triggered our Timing, Tmg Buy Signal and our computer has calculated a 12-month target of ~$1,150. LLY earnings announcement is due on Oct. 30, and we expect the usual pop after good earnings. Our report below shows the Buy signal in the Tmg column. It also shows another short-term, timing signal in the Stop & Reverse or SAR columns which was just triggered on Friday, shown with a B in the Friday or F column. These signals are similar to the Confirmed buy signal shown in our last article on LLY.

Here is our daily portfolio Watchlist and you can see LLY is on the list with these short-term Buy signals in the Tmg and SAR columns:

Note on the above report that LLY also has a Buy signal in the Fundamental or Fund column. We will check that out below with SA, but right now, we want to turn to the weekly chart and the technical Buy signals.

On the chart below, you can see the vertical, blue line, technical Buy signal triggered by the Full Stochastic or Full STO signal. In addition, you can see the bullish Chaikin money flow signal indicating Demand taking price higher. At the bottom of the chart is the ADX Demand signal. Also notice the uptrend in performance vs. the Index. You can see a bounce after dropping down to test that uptrend that indicates LLY is outperforming the Index.

There are also negative signals helping to create the Supply that is triggering our buy on weakness signals. At the top of the chart is our proprietary Stocks In Demand or SID signal. You can see it has a regular pattern of wide swings from Hold to Buy and back to Hold. We think the reason for this is that many portfolio managers have too much LLY in their portfolio because of the big runup in price. They are probably forced to sell a stock they love every time price moves higher. The buyers, of course, are ETFs, Index funds, hedge funds and small investors.

Here is our weekly chart showing our technical Buy signals, except for the MACD and SID signals:

Now we want to return to our Fund Buy signal mentioned above and shown on our Daily Index Beaters report in the Fund column. We do our due diligence on this signal by checking with SA analysts and Quant ratings. Our SID Hold rating is in agreement with the SA Quant Hold rating, so we are comfortable with that, but we expect earnings will pop our SID signal to a Buy. Both SA and Wall St. analysts rate LLY as a Buy, and we certainly find that supporting the Buy signal in our Fund column shown in the report above.

The SA Quant scores for Growth, Momentum and Profitability are very good for LLY. However, poor scores on Valuation and Revisions are problematic. The forward PE of 68 is difficult to accept and allows for no possibility of any downward revisions. LLY is priced to perfection, and that makes it risky. That may also explain the big drop in our SID score from a Buy signal to a Hold signal.

Poor scores for Valuation and Revisions would prompt many portfolio managers to sell into strength. So even if we get a pop on this earnings report, we will probably see selling on the good news, as we have seen in the past.

Nevertheless, there are other buyers out there who will still be attracted to LLY and the higher target our computer projects for the year ahead. Index funds and sector ETFs usually do not use fundamentals to buy. They only use Demand for the Index to buy LLY because it is part of the Index or ETF.

data.title: Fluor: Stock Looks Bullish To End 2024 And To Begin 2025
data.description:
I'm continuing my coverage of Fluor Corporation (NYSE:FLR) as the company continues to get new contract awards. I last wrote about Fluor in January 2024 where I pointed out how being awarded a steady flow of contracts would have a positive effect on the company. My thesis played out nicely as FLR stock is up 48% since the article was published as compared to the S&P 500's (SPY) gain of 21% over the same time period. I now see Fluor having a strong finish to 2024 which I expect will continue in 2025.

Fluor has a positive outlook as we finish 2024 and head into 2025 with new contract awards. The stock's valuation is still reasonable and Fluor is expected to grow revenue and earnings at a strong pace in 2025. In fact, Fluor's expected earnings growth is expected to be higher at 13% in 2025 as compared to about 6% in 2024. The double-digit EPS growth rate can drive the stock for continued strong gains in 2025.

New Contract Awards as Growth Catalysts

Fluor's steady flow of new contracts demonstrates that demand remains strong for the company's engineering, construction, and related solutions. The most recent contract was awarded to Fluor in August for a $1.4 billion cost plus fixed-fee modification to a previously rewarded contract for fiscal 2025. This contract is for U.S. naval nuclear propulsion work at the Navel Nuclear Laboratory. Work under this contract will be performed in Pittsburgh, PA; Schenectady, NY and Idaho Falls, ID.

FLR was awarded a contract in July from RoPower Nuclear for the next phase of design and engineering of a modular reactor power plant in Romania. The project has a goal of producing carbon-free power which is one of Romania's clean energy initiatives. The amount of the contract was not disclosed in the press release. However, small modular reactors can cost between $50 million and $3 billion depending on the size. This one is designed to be a 462 megawatt reactor which is on the larger end of the scale for small modular reactors. So, the amount of the deal could be closer to $3 billion.

In June, Fluor was awarded a long-term contract with the potential for $30 billion over 20 years. This contract was from the U.S. Department of Energy National Nuclear Security Administration for a joint venture with Fluor, BWX Technologies' (BWXT) subsidiary PanTeXas Deterrence LLC, SOC, and Texas A&M University. This joint venture is for the management and operations of the Pantex Plant located outside of Amarillo, TX. The initial term of the contract is for 5 years, but there is the option to extend it for 3 additional five year periods. Pantex is a key nuclear production plant which is responsible for maintaining the safety, security, and effectiveness of the U.S. nuclear weapons stockpile.

Fluor was awarded a $409 million contract in April from the U.S. Airforce Installation Contracting Agency. This contract is for pavement and transportation support in Tinian, Commonwealth of the Northern Mariana Islands. This is a cost-plus, fixed fee task order with a 60 month period of performance. The project is designed to help strengthen the Air Force's deterrence capabilities.

In April, FLR was awarded a 7-year contract extension from SunCoke Energy for an undisclosed amount. This is for Fluor to support the efficient delivery of coke to SunCoke Energy's steel-making customers. The contract involved SunCoke's five coke plants in the United States.

Also in April, Fluor was awarded a position on the Global Contingency Services Multiple Award Contract III from the U.S. Naval Facilities Engineering Systems Command. FLR was chosen as one of six companies for the indefinite delivery & indefinite quantity contract. FLR is eligible to compete for specific tasks for an amount up to $2 billion for up to 102 months. The contract is to provide timely short-term support services due to natural disasters, military actions, or humanitarian efforts. This would take place in various areas throughout the world.

In March, Fluor was selected for the Hanford Tank Waste Operations & Closure joint venture along with BWX Technologies, and Amentum (AMTM). These companies were selected by the U.S. Department of Energy. The joint venture is to execute the Hanford Integrated Tank Disposition Contract which has an estimated max rate of $45 billion over 10 years. The joint venture is for environmental management operations at the Hanford Site in Washington state.

In February, Fluor was awarded a $525.6 million contract from FEMA. The contract is for recovery services under the Public Assistance Technical Assistance Contracts in the East Zone region. The contract is for one year with four one-year option periods. Under the contract, Fluor is to provide assistance for presidentially declared emergencies/disasters.

All of these developments show that Fluor continues to get a steady stream of contracts for ongoing growth. The strong demand for services have been driving Fluor's margins higher. Fluor's service margins for its traditional EPCM (engineering, procurement and construction management) business have been in the 20% range in 2024. Fluor's shift to an asset light business also helps drive margins higher. This helped drive Q2 2024 net income margin to 4% over the 1.55% net income margin from Q2 2023. The increases in net income margin can drive strong earnings growth for the remainder of 2024 and for 2025.

Fluor also made improvements in its return metrics since I conveyed that weakness in my last article. Fluor's TTM ROE increased from 5.9% in January 2024 to the current TTM ROE of 19%. The ROIC also increased from 6% to 7.5% and the ROA increased from 2.4% to 6% over the same time period. These higher returns can help drive earnings growth going forward.

Valuation

Fluor is trading at 16.9x expected EPS of $3.26 for 2025 (consensus). This is higher than the 14.3x forward PE that FLR was trading at when I wrote my last article back in January. The stock did make strong gains in 2024 which increased the PE. However, FLR still trades below the Engineering & Construction industry's forward PE of 23.5x. So, while FLR's valuation is higher since my last article, the stock is still attractively valued below its industry. This valuation still allows for some PE expansion as Fluor continues to grow.

The consensus estimate EPS of $3.26 for 2025 represents a 13% gain over the expected EPS of $2.88 for 2024. Fluor's strong margin increases can drive EPS growth to achieve these estimates. The increased margins are also driven by FLR's revenue growth. FLR's revenue is expected to increase by 12.5% in 2024 and by about 9% in 2025.

Technical Perspective

I used FLR's weekly chart above to provide a long-term multiple-year perspective. We can see the stock is a nice uptrend with some healthy pullbacks along the way. The stock did touch the overbought area just above 70 on the RSI. However, it is possible for the price to go deeper into overbought territory. Investors should be aware that a pullback could occur at any time. That is nothing to worry about as long as Fluor's fundamentals remain strong.

The stock does tend to pullback after reaching overbought or close to overbought areas. These pullbacks have been followed by strong increases which led to higher highs, driven by FLR's revenue & earnings growth.

The MACD indicator (bottom of the chart) shows positive momentum as the blue MACD line crossed above the red signal line. At the same time, the histogram turned from red to green. So, there could be continued increases in the stock before a pullback occurs. It can be difficult to gauge where the stock will go in the short-term. So, investors should have a long-term perspective as FLR's stock should correlate approximately with earnings growth with the potential for a boost from PE expansion.

Risks to the Investment Thesis

The biggest risk for the company is a slowdown in demand for Fluor's service offerings. FLR's traditional EPCM business is what has been driving the company's margin increases and overall growth. A significant decrease in government spending could lessen demand for some of Fluor's potential contracts. This includes United States and foreign government spending.

Fluor's revenue highly depends on a stream of new contracts. There is no guarantee that FLR will continue to receive a steady flow of new contracts. This depends on FLR's customers' willingness to direct money to these types of projects.

Fluor's Long-Term Outlook

With the United States and other countries returning to a rate cutting cycle, this could stimulate higher spending on some of the large projects that Fluor specializes in. The current positive momentum that Fluor is achieving is likely to drive strong growth for the remainder of 2024 and for 2025. Higher margins are likely to drive earnings growth as demand for Fluor's high margin service business remains strong.

Investors should watch for Fluor to be awarded new contracts in the future to gauge the health of the demand for the company's offerings.

The stock has the potential to increase at a pace that is higher than FLR's earnings growth. I think that there is still some room for multiple expansion as FLR still trades below its industry.

data.title: Hyundai Drives Value Amid Industry Shifts (Ratings Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

Hyundai Motor Company (OTCPK:HYMTF) is one of those companies that flies under the radar, quietly going about its business while most of us are distracted by shinier things. Yet it’s easily one of my favorite stocks to own. They’ve managed to crack the code on making cars that offer solid performance and sleek design—all without asking you to remortgage your house to afford one.

But Hyundai’s not just about cars. In 2023, I wrote about how their AI-powered robots are set to alter the playing field along with making significant strides in hydrogen technology, and sustainable mobility. Now, as we get closer to the Q3 2024 earnings, coming out on 10/24, a few things have changed since I last updated and downgraded the stock to a ‘Hold.’ Even with that downgrade, the stock has still kept pace with the 5-6% gain we’ve seen in the S&P 500 (SPY).

In this update, I’m going to take another look at Hyundai’s valuation, dig into the S&P Global warning about the challenges coming for the auto industry, and highlight a few key partnerships. I’ll also break down Hyundai's September and Q3 2024 sales reports to get a better idea of what we can expect from the upcoming earnings. Finally, I’ll update my current rating.

Record Sales Amid Industry Challenges

In Q3 2024, Hyundai Motor America hit a new retail sales record, even with all the economic and industry challenges in play. They moved 210,971 units, up 5% from the same time last year, making it one of the strongest third quarters in the company’s history.

A big part of this growth came from the rising demand for hybrid and electric vehicles, especially models like the Kona and Ioniq 5. These form an essential part of Hyundai’s effort to appeal to an increasing number of green car buyers. Their hybrid lineup really stood out, with a 36% jump in sales in September alone, showing Hyundai’s ability to balance traditional gas engines with a serious push towards electrification.

September Sales Challenges

Despite the solid performance overall, Hyundai’s September sales took a bit of a tumble, down 9% compared to September 2023. It's worth noting that this dip had less to do with any grand market trends and more to do with some rather unremarkable technicalities—fewer selling days and Labor Day deals spilling over into August.

CEO Randy Parker pointed out that while these short-term hiccups hit the September numbers, the overall outlook stays very positive. Hyundai is still on track for a record-breaking year, thanks in large part to the continued success of the IONIQ 5 electric vehicle, which saw a 30% jump in retail sales during Q3.

Hyundai's Long-Term EV Strategy

Hyundai’s recent market success is no fluke—it ties in nicely with their global game plan, which targets selling 5.55 million vehicles a year by 2030, with 2 million of those being electric. To make this happen, Hyundai has been quietly but steadily building up its hybrid and electric lineup, and the numbers back it up in the strong sales of the IONIQ 5 and the record-breaking numbers for the Tucson and Santa Fe hybrids.

What’s particularly smart here is that Hyundai understands the simple truth that not all consumers are on the same page—some see hybrids as a kind of gateway vehicle, while others are diving in, fully committed to the electric cause. Meanwhile, Hyundai’s big investments in production, like the new Metaplant America EV facility in Georgia, underline the company’s focus on ramping up EV production and boosting its presence in the U.S. market.

Challenges in the Broader Auto Market

That said, Hyundai’s success comes with its fair share of industry-wide challenges. A report from S&P Global highlights that the U.S. auto industry is staring down limited growth and rising economic pressures. The report flags some concerns, like modest credit deterioration, especially for lower-rated auto suppliers, and predicts U.S. auto sales will likely stay flat in 2024 at around 15.5 million units and growth is only expected to tick up by 1% to 2% in 2025 and 2026.

On top of that, rising unemployment could cut into consumers’ ability to spend, making it harder for automakers to keep profit margins up. To make matters trickier, average transaction prices for new vehicles are expected to drop by 6% to 8% by 2025, as automakers roll out more incentives to attract budget-conscious buyers. While Hyundai’s lower-priced EVs, like the IONIQ 5, could benefit from this trend, it might also squeeze margins, forcing the company to focus on scaling production and cutting costs to stay profitable.

Slower Growth in the EV Market

S&P Global’s report also points out some bumps in the road for the electric vehicle market. While EV and plug-in hybrid sales are expected to hit 20% of the total market by 2026, growth has slowed in 2024. Many models are dealing with rising inventories and increased competition.

Take Tesla, for example—they’ve struggled to hold onto market share, even after multiple price cuts on the Model 3. This highlights a broader issue: profitability parity between electric and traditional vehicles probably won’t happen until after 2027, especially for automakers that can’t hit economies of scale. For Hyundai, this means they’ll need to keep pushing to cut costs and ramp up production efficiency as they go head-to-head with both established players like Tesla and new competition from China.

Impact of the 2024 U.S. Election

The 2024 U.S. presidential election adds another wrinkle to Hyundai’s strategic planning. Depending on who comes out on top, the regulatory landscape could shift in some pretty significant ways, particularly when it comes to emissions standards, trade policies, and those ever-tempting federal incentives for electric vehicles. According to S&P Global Mobility, a few different paths could unfold based on who wins.

If the Republicans win, we could see emissions regulations more or less put on ice for model years 2028 through 2032. Naturally, this would slow the march toward electrification, forcing Hyundai to put a bit more love into their internal combustion engine and hybrid models, while perhaps easing off the gas (or, in this case, electricity) on their big EV push in the U.S.

And then there’s the matter of federal funding. The Inflation Reduction Act (IRA), which has been helping buyers justify the price tag of EVs through generous incentives, could see its budget slashed. If that happens, demand for Hyundai’s electric lineup—especially the IONIQ series—might take a bit of a hit.

On the flip side, if the Democrats manage to hang onto the White House, the regulatory environment would likely stay favorable for EV growth. In this scenario, Hyundai would likely enjoy the continued benefits of federal incentives, which would help speed up the transition to EVs. We might also see tougher emissions standards coming down the pipeline, nudging automakers to keep cranking out cleaner, more efficient cars.

The way I see it, no matter the outcome, Hyundai’s flexibility and its investments in U.S. production, like the Metaplant America facility, put the company in a strong position to handle changing regulations and evolving consumer demands.

Strategic Partnerships for Technological Advancements

Hyundai, with its characteristic knack for overcoming difficulties, has also been teaming up with the right players to boost its tech capabilities. In September 2024, Hyundai and General Motors did what all sensible companies do when facing a complex future—they signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) to see if they could help each other out. The idea is to explore potential collaborations in areas like electric and hydrogen-powered vehicles.

By pooling their resources, both companies can reduce costs, particularly in securing those all-important materials like batteries and steel—key ingredients in the clean-energy vehicle recipe. This collaboration is expected to speed up Hyundai’s ability to launch new models, while also tackling the rather pesky issue of making electric vehicles affordable on a large scale.

Hyundai's Autonomous Vehicle Ambitions

Hyundai’s venture into autonomous driving is another rather key piece of its long-term growth puzzle. In October 2024, they joined forces with Alphabet’s Waymo, aiming to install Waymo’s sixth-generation autonomous driving technology into Hyundai’s IONIQ 5 SUV. It opens up a nice window for Hyundai to make a bigger splash in the mobility-as-a-service world, with plans to have Waymo-enabled IONIQ 5s cruising around by late 2025.

Valuation and Final Takeaway

To value HYMTF, I took a slightly different route this time, opting to use Hyundai’s compound annual growth rate (OTC:CAGR) of 4.55% from 2014 to TTM rather than the Adjusted (Operating) Earnings Growth Rate.

I also factored in its earnings per share (EPS) of 37.47 and a rather low price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio of 4.59 that suggests the stock is quite undervalued, especially when you consider that the market typically shoots for a P/E closer to 15. So, if Hyundai can keep its earnings on track, there’s plenty of room for growth here.

With an EPS of $37.47, Hyundai’s earnings are quite robust—especially when you realize the stock is hanging out in the low $60s. That said, the 4.55% growth rate might not exactly set anyone’s hair on fire, and those chasing faster returns might well pass it by. But when it comes to fundamentals, Hyundai’s valuation should be appealing.

All things considered, I’m upgrading Hyundai to a ‘Buy’: the stock is clearly undervalued, with a strong EPS and a modest P/E ratio, which leaves plenty of space for growth, provided the earnings keep holding up. And while the industry itself isn’t without its challenges, Hyundai’s strides in EV, robotics, and its strategic partnerships mean it’s positioned rather nicely for long-term success—even if a few short-term uncertainties pop up along the way.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Entegris: The Road Ahead Has Become Misty
data.description:
Entegris (NASDAQ:ENTG), a supplier of materials and process solutions for the semiconductor and other industries, was powering higher earlier in the year, which made it relatively easy to guess the most likely direction of the stock. However, the road ahead has become harder to figure out for ENTG with the appearance of several headwinds, which a leading supplier of lithography systems recently reiterated. The stock has struggled lately, which may portend a possible drop to new lows. ENTG needs to turn it around for the bulls to not get discouraged, especially since ENTG is not without its flaws. Why will be covered next.

ENTG has struggled in October

A previous article from February 2024 mentioned how the stock had rallied higher because it was able to neutralize the negatives by pointing out the positives. Upwards adjustments to ENTG’s financial model, for instance, negated any blowback the stock could have gotten from ENTG missing expectations with its quarterly guidance.

The stock continued higher in an uptrend by moving higher in an ascending channel, but the article noted that a pullback was likely not far away. In addition, ENTG remained heavily leveraged, mostly due to all the debt it acquired as a result of the CMC acquisition, just when the industry entered a downturn. In the end, ENTG was rated a hold.

The chart above shows why the article dodged a bullet by not buying into the rally, even though there was reason to. Notice how the stock was in an uptrend earlier in the year, including when the previous article was written, but ENTG was unable to push higher after early March. ENTG, for instance, has lost about a quarter of its market since the article was written.

The stock experienced a succession of selloffs that wiped out all the early gains and then some. The first selloff was in April, followed by an even larger one in July, and ENTG may be in the process of another one in October. ENTG is now down 13.9% YTD as a result. In addition, there are a couple of interesting things worth mentioning, looking at the charts.

What the charts suggest the stock is likely to do in the short term

For starters, note how the stock topped out twice at close to the same price point months apart. The stock topped out at $146.91 on March 8, followed by a decline that resulted in a low of $120.36 on April 22. ENTG turned north to hit a high of $147.57 on July 12, but then fell off a cliff to a low of $95.54 on August 5. The March high of $146.91 and the July high of $147.57 are four months apart, yet just $0.66 separate the two.

This could be considered a double top, a bearish sign, which proved to be warranted with the drop that followed the second top. The July collapse resulted in a low of $95.54 on August 5, but a bounce got the stock to as high as $119.95 on August 26, only to falter again afterwards. What is interesting here is that the August 26 high of $119.95 is very close to the aforementioned April 22 low of $120.36.

In both instances, the stock proceeded to turn around and move in the opposite direction. What used to be support tends to become resistance and vice versa, which is what seems to have happened here. In other words, resistance is now in place above the stock. On the other hand, while resistance is in place, it seems the same can be said of support.

The previous chart shows how the stock bounced following a drop in the stock from the July high of $147.57 to the August low of $95.54. The July 2024 high of $147.57 was in turn preceded by an extended move upwards that started with the low of $62.24 in December 2022. More importantly, the 38.2% Fibonacci retracement of $62.24 to $147.57 is $94.81. This is close to where the stock bounced at $95.54, which sheds light on why the stock bounced where it did. ENTG hit support.

If the above is correct, then ENTG finds itself between support and resistance. This could make large extended moves difficult, whether up or down, especially in the short term, unless some catalyst appears that powers the stock through resistance, or alternatively, through support. The path of least resistance is for sideways action in the stock.

Still, the price action has favored the downside in October, since the stock is down for the month. The stock has been sliding for most of October and if that continues, a challenge of support could happen. Support is holding, but bulls would probably be more at ease if the stock showed more strength than it has for much of October.

What is weighing on ENTG?

Up until early 2024 ENTG was doing okay with the stock in an uptrend that can be traced back to December 2022. This uptrend in the stock was preceded by an almost year-long decline in the stock in 2022, which was caused by a number of contributing factors, including a downturn in the industry. The resulting drop in demand caused a decline in the top and the bottom line, which came at a very bad time for ENTG because of the untimely acquisition of CMC Materials in mid-2022.

ENTG had leveraged itself heavily to finance the $5.7B acquisition, but servicing debt is much harder to do in a downturn. ENTG took a number of measures in response, which included selling assets to raise proceeds with which to pay off debt. This included the electronic chemical business and the PIM business.

Measures like these helped the stock turn it around. Improved quarterly results and the upgraded financial model mentioned earlier also helped push the stock higher. However, the external environment has become less favorable in 2024, which led to the collapse in July. The initial drop was triggered by reports of possibly new trade restrictions on China relating to semiconductors and everything related to it, which presumably includes equipment and materials from suppliers like ENTG.

Recent reports that some companies like ASML (ASML), seen as a canary in the coal mine, are feeling the adverse effects of trade restrictions imposed by the U.S. government, which included bookings that was less than half the number expected. A drop in bookings for lithography systems from ASML could be a sign companies intend to reduce spending on new production capacity, including new fabs, which is no good for ENTG due to its large exposure to fab construction.

The above caused ENTG to drop by 7.9% on October 15 and not without reason. Reduced trade implies reduced demand. This is something ENTG can do without because it needs all the demand it can get, especially with the debt it has to service because more trade means more sales and an increased ability to service debt.

ENTG itself is still chugging along

Adding to the uncertainty is that not everyone is in the same boat. For instance, some chipmakers like Intel (INTC) have stated they need to cut back on capex, but others like TSMC (TSM) believe they will need to increase capex. It does not help that ENTG itself has been sending mixed signals. For instance, ENTG believes the industry continues to improve overall following the downturn. From the Q2 earnings call:

“2024 continues to be a transition year for the semiconductor market. Industry inventories are normalizing, and fab utilization rates are broadly improving. These recent trends validate that the industry reached the bottom of the cycle in the first quarter of this year. We expect the market will continue to gradually recover in the second half of this year and will accelerate entering 2025.”

Source: ENTG earnings call

On the other hand, ENTG pointed out the recovery was going slower than expected. The outlook was thus lowered.

“we now expect our sales will be approximately $3.3 billion. This modest reduction in our 2024 sales outlook primarily reflects slightly slower-than-expected market recovery in the back half of the year and the negative impact of foreign exchange versus our original assumptions.”

This mixed messaging offset the progress ENTG made. Quarterly earnings, for instance, are on the rise. In the most recent or Q2 FY2024 report, non-GAAP EPS improved to $0.71 on revenue of $812.65M, as shown below. Keep in mind, ENTG divested a number of units. Not only is this skewing the YoY comparisons, but the gains in gross margin can be partially attributed to the absence of units with lower margins.

One of, if not the biggest flaw ENTG has is the amount of debt it has. The good news is that ENTG continues to make progress here. Long-term debt decreased to $4,122.2M in Q2 FY2024, down from $4,577.1M at the start of FY2024, and partially offset by cash and cash equivalents totaling $320M. In comparison, total debt was above $6B two years ago. Most of the reduction can be attributed to the sale of assets.

What will FY2025 bring?

ENTG looks to be on track for non-GAAP EPS of about $3.15 on revenue of $3.3B by the end of FY2024. This would give ENTG a P/E ratio of 32.8x with the stock at $103.23 as of October 18, which is in line with where ENTG has traded on average in recent years. However, figuring FY2025 out is harder because of the uncertainty to the outlook.

If we just extrapolate into the future and assume the recent sequential improvement in quarterly results continues, then FY2025 EPS is estimated to come in at around $4.25. This would lower the P/E ratio to 24.3x. If correct, then ENTG would be in a good position to hit the targets laid out in its financial model, which calls for non-GAAP EPS of $5.00 on revenue of $4.2B in FY2026.

Keep in mind that if demand comes in weaker than expected for whatever reason, whether due to export restrictions or a slower recovery due to companies cutting back on capex, then that might shift the timetable further out. FY2025 is generally expected to be a year of growth for the industry, including by ENTG itself, but something like new export restrictions, if introduced, could add another twist to that dynamic.

Investor takeaways

ENTG continues to slowly get out of the hole it found itself. Total debt has been reduced by about a third or $2B in the last two years, although at the cost of ENTG becoming a smaller company due to the sale of assets. On the other hand, further progress could be harder because there are no more assets that are easily expendable. The low-hanging fruit is gone. If ENTG wants to reduce debt, it likely won’t be through the sale of assets like before. It will have to do it through earnings.

Earnings have been inching higher, and they are expected to continue to do so next year. However, the growth trajectory could be altered by export restrictions. There are already reports from the likes of ASML that export restrictions are starting to affect the numbers negatively. More export restrictions could impact the way 2025 plays out for ENTG. The recovery has been slower than expected, but it could slow even more.

The charts suggest the stock is most likely going sideways in the short term. If ENTG wants to resume the uptrend from early 2024, it will have to break through resistance. On the other hand, there also appears to be some support in place to prevent ENTG from falling much further. Keep in mind both support and resistance can be breached, especially if a catalyst appears like say the U.S. government announcing a new set of export restrictions.

I am therefore neutral on ENTG with the above in mind. The overall environment has deteriorated for ENTG, certainly in comparison to earlier in the year. The prudent move here may be to wait on the sidelines to see whether the stock breaks through resistance or support, which would show whether the bulls or the bears are in the driver seat. At this time, neither of them seem to have an advantage over the other. ENTG could be on hold while this persists.

data.title: Alpha Picks Weekly Market Recap
data.description:
Global Chip Stocks Fall: ASML Forecast Impacts Market Dynamics

The Nasdaq slid 1% on Tuesday, and the S&P 500 halted a two-day rally after Dutch semiconductor giant ASML Holding N.V. (ASML) reported disappointing earnings and fell 14%. Wall Street closed in the red Tuesday, with global chip stocks losing over $420B in market value following ASML's forecast for 2025, which came in below expectations. ASML's unexpected revision of its net sales forecast for 2025 and disappointing third quarter bookings sent shockwaves through the semiconductor industry, with Asian and European chip stocks continuing their slide Wednesday. Japan's Tokyo Electron was one of Wednesday's biggest losers, falling nearly 10%, with ASML down another 4%.

ASML's focus on AI is strong, yet challenges in other segments, including logic and memory, have slowed recovery efforts. These developments raise concerns about the semiconductor supply chain and the impact of export restrictions. ASML's unfavorable outlook for 2025 isn't the only headwind facing the semiconductor industry. Recent export restrictions imposed by the U.S. and Dutch governments have adversely impacted ASML's business in China, reducing its revenue from the region. China's past robust demand for semiconductor technology has been a significant growth driver for companies like ASML, which now anticipates China to account for only 20% of its total revenue next year, a sharp decline from previous quarters. Additionally, the semiconductor market's volatility was exacerbated by ASML's report, which showed a substantial shortfall in third quarter net orders. This has created ripple effects, causing a noteworthy $420 billion drop in market value across global semiconductor stocks, including those of giants like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD), and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM). The longer recovery timelines for market segments such as logic and memory further pressure the industry outlook.

Markets are constantly influenced by global economic and geopolitical events. If you're looking for a tool that simplifies stock picking by analyzing financial data to highlight stocks with the strongest investment fundamentals, Alpha Picks offers a more efficient approach to smarter investing. This service leverages Seeking Alpha's powerful quantitative stock-picking system, delivering two data-driven stock picks each month, helping investors save time and focus on high-quality opportunities.

Geopolitical Events

The geopolitical landscape remains tense as Israel's offensive into Lebanon and Iran's countermeasures have heightened the risk of conflict escalation in the Middle East. The potential impact on global oil supply adds to the complexity, though analysts suggest the focus will likely remain on Iran's military rather than energy infrastructure. Nevertheless, the threat of rising tensions persists over oil markets, which now rely more on production from non-OPEC nations to weather geopolitical storms.

Despite global tensions, modern-day oil markets remain resilient

Globally, the resilience of the Middle East conflict's influence on oil prices illustrates modern-day market tenacity. Despite initial surges driven by uncertainties, prices have shown quick retractions post-conflict, echoing the responses of the post-Russian invasion of Ukraine and recent escalations in Israel.

GICS Sector Performance

This week, Financials was the top-performing sector boosted by positive earnings surprises for big banks and the potential for another rate cut in November. Utilities reclaimed higher positioning, which is consistent with their performance year-to-date. Utilities have outperformed in 2024 due to a confluence of factors, including the sector's defensive attributes in a volatile year, as well as AI-related power demand. This week, Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) announced agreements to develop nuclear projects to fuel their data centers, boosting expectations for the Utilities sector yet again.

Industrials is the best-performing sector over the last month

The Energy sector finished as the worst-performing sector for the week, erasing much of the gains it had made in the wake of Iran's October 1st missile attack on Israel.

Crude Oil and Chinese Economic Stimulus

There is one corner of the world that may offer some upside pressure to energy prices - China. The country is the world's largest crude oil importer. With the nation recently announcing stimulus measures, this could drive demand for imports and the commodity - see chart below. Seeking Alpha's Quant team already identified top Q4 energy stocks that may benefit from these developments.

Value vs. Growth

Growth stocks continue to outpace their value counterparts amidst declining interest rates, spurred by anticipation of the Federal Reserve's easing monetary policy. The Technology and Financial sectors spearhead this growth, capitalizing on increased innovation and positive earnings revisions. However, broader macroeconomic uncertainties provide a backdrop of caution.

Large-cap growth (IWF) continued to outperform Large-Cap Value (IWD) over the last month, but the latter has slightly outperformed over the last week as traders consider the potential shift from growth to value stocks.

Heightened optimism and hefty valuations have prompted some rotation, despite Big Tech reaping the benefits of the AI and ChatGPT-fueled frenzies that added $8.2T to six of the Magnificent Seven stocks. As highlighted last week, ongoing pressures from antitrust scrutiny and uncertain demand for tech hardware weigh on growth stocks' outlook, prompting increasingly favorable sentiment toward value stocks as investors navigate the markets more cautiously.

Over the last month, Growth outperformed Value across all segments

Declining interest rates typically bolster value stocks by enhancing their relative appeal versus growth stocks with elevated P/E ratios. The U.S. economy remains resilient, despite economic forecasts indicating a GDP deceleration and climbing recession odds. Value stocks in slowing economies tend to become more attractive as they offer dividends and stability - a potential shift to support a rotation toward value equities.

Crypto

Cryptocurrency markets experienced a surge, with Bitcoin leading digital assets to a two-month-high following significant ETF inflows. Meanwhile, Tesla's transfer of $760 million worth of Bitcoin to unidentified wallets raised speculation regarding its crypto holdings strategy and its ongoing influence on crypto sentiments and broader market interpretation.

Cryptocurrencies Update: Bitcoin Benefits from Upbeat Financial Market Sentiment

Regulatory challenges continue to pose headwinds for the crypto market by increasing uncertainty. Yet, Bitcoin's resilience remains tested. Increased pressure from legal obstacles exacerbates existing volatility linked to geopolitical tensions, factors contributing to a challenging landscape for cryptocurrencies.

Currency Performance

The US dollar experienced broadly positive movements for the week, making further gains against the yen and most major currencies. The European Central Bank (ECB) cut interest rates by 0.25% on Thursday, the third time this year, edging up the dollar's gains against the euro for the month. The dollar has been aided by US economic resilience, with strengthening employment and cooling inflation.

Sterling fell on Wednesday, following the release of economic data that showed British inflation slowed more than expected in September, making room for another potential rate cut by the Bank of England.

Geopolitical tensions and global economic conditions continue to influence investor sentiment. Heightened yields on U.S. Treasuries further exacerbated this trend, making the dollar a more attractive safe haven.

Chart of the Week - Are Stocks Focusing on Presidential Betting Markets?

With earnings season in full swing, the S&P 500 recently had its 46th instance of a record high this year. That is not the only metric hitting new peaks of late. According to PredictIt U.S. presidential betting markets, Donald Trump has continued to pull ahead of Kamala Harris.

As of October 17, Trump's price stood at 55 cents while Harris traded at 48. The spread stood at +7, the most elevated since the aftermath of the Biden-Trump debate. There has been an increasingly positive relationship between the stock market and this probability spread.
